It's over. I can't have one. I mean there's got to be like no landlines left on the planet before I dare have one. It's too big of a switch, right? And then it starts to have everything questioned. It's sort of like, you know, I can't, it's beyond me how anybody could stay Catholic after the fish deal. So that would have been enough for me, you know. Thirteen years of eating fish sticks when I wanted a hamburger because I burn in hell forever if I have a hamburger on Friday. We changed our mind. Go ahead, have a big Mac. What day are you watching? Is that Thursday instead of Friday because you didn't want to help? I mean, but seeing the change in that to me would have been enough to, you know, and I fear that kind of change. So I think there's a lot to be said for consistency and there's great risk, you know, in inconsistency. If you look at John Edwards' problem, so why a story fails. So John Edwards has a great story and he's not getting any traction with it. Oh, yeah, his whole story of I'm from the little guy and dad worked in a mill and I worked in, well, okay, but that's not the way he tells the story. And I worked in a mill and, you know, and the story's fine. The problem is he's the guy telling it. He looks wrong and the 26,000 square foot house and the 12 cars and the, you know, the $400 haircut follows that story everywhere it goes. And the inconsistency destroys its value. So I've always been pretty cognizant of it, you know. So for example, on 93, I mean, so like when I talk about brand, I'm very, very careful. I give that a lot of thought. Anytime I'm going to have a discussion about brand building because my core position is don't spend any money on brand, right? Spend all your money on direct response. So I got to be real careful about being inconsistent about that. The internet. I generally can't show how much I know. I know less than most of you, but I know more than most of you think I know, right? But I can't really show all that because I've taken a core position on that. Pretty much unless I was dead, I had to be at that super conference because calling sick is completely inconsistent with every message I convey. The corollary of that, by the way, is a lot of comment and all that. I bought a lot of, you know, impressed that he was there under those circumstances. But, you know, couldn't do it. Couldn't do it as much as I might have liked. So you got to think about, and you got to be careful about the box you put yourself in, that it's a box you want to stay in. It's why I've never liked the personal development business and got away from it as quick as I could because it imposes things upon you that teaching and representing other sorts of things do not. Sort of a higher level of perfection, if you will, that's pretty hard to live up to. Okay, 14. Here's a biggie. And we'll spend a little time on this one. This is called the principle of place. And this means that what you present to the people you want to influence always has to be thought of in part as place. So there's really three things about it. One is, you have to have a door they walk through to get to you. There's a point of entry that feels physical. Like I am coming from place A, world A, into place B, world B, where everything is different. And so you've got to think about everything that you present to them as a place they are arriving at and hopefully then feeling real good about being a part of. Second, the environment has to be completely different from the outside world and the world from whence they come. So if everybody's real buttoned up in their world, your world maybe is not buttoned up or vice versa, usually for many of us it is more about a pessimism, optimism. It's a world where everybody is excited about what they're doing instead of bored and fatigued and going through the motions, but it's a place. And of course it has to be consistent with your positions. So I've given you a few examples. I mean Martha Stewart is about a place of class and luxury and attention to detail that is still affordable and accessible. So it's not completely out of reach, but it's more civil than the environment most women paying attention to Martha Stewart find themselves in and they can make it happen for a brief period of time, but they've got kids and cats and they don't have servants and so it's hard to keep it, but it's a place they wish they could be in. Howard Stern is all about animal house. I mean Howard Stern provides a place where grown men can go and completely misbehave and deal with fart jokes and treat women badly and it's all okay. And there's some part of a whole lot of guys who still wish they were there. And so he provides that place for them. Limbaugh provides a place for people to be superior to other people. It's a place where you get to be smarter than everybody around you and you get to be right when most of the time people are treating you as if you are wrong. So you have to think about this environmental picture that you are presenting to people through everything you write, everything you talk about, every story you tell, every testimonial you present to them in the context of a place. On page 95 it's said many different ways of course, but this particular quote, the most powerful narcotic is the promise of belonging. We largely typically the most valuable customers to our customers for everybody in this room in one way, shape or form are people who feel like odd man out where they are. So they either feel underappreciated or disrespected or underpaid, misunderstood, criticized. You know Bill has this saying that the entrepreneur is the most the loneliest person on the planet. The truth is the engineer, the bus driver, the martial arts instructor, the pretty much they all feel like they are lonely. Nobody gets them. When you provide a place where people get them or they are understood, you immediately have enormous influence over them. And so if you look at a lot of my writing, there's an example I was going to show you. Yeah, it's a place where you're okay, right? Where you're right. Where in other environments you're not. And you know people want to be accepted, people want to be understood, people want to feel welcome, they want to feel like they've arrived at the right place. And one way or another you will hear that, especially those of you that do seminars or coaching programs, you'll hear people sort of enunciate that. You know, oh I finally found a home. Oh I finally found a place where it's okay to think like this. And so a lot of language is about communicating that. If you go back and read pages 30 and 31 of the No BS Business Success Book, it's a whole little riff on everybody in your normal life criticizes you as a workaholic and then on to why they're wrong and why you're right and why workaholism is a virtue. And so in this place, you know, there's a thing in the Superman comics.
